content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
May 2, 2016 
Brent Fremming, MA 
Staff Analyst 
County of Fresno 
Department of Public Works and Planning 
Resources Division 
2220 Tulare Street, 6th Floor 
Fresno, CA 93721-2127 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-16-068 
Dear Mr. Fremming: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This advice is based on the facts provided in your request. 
The Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it 
renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71; Section 83114.) Because your letter seeks 
general information, we are treating your request as one for infonnal assistance.2 
QUESTION 
Is the West County Solid Waste Planning Committee a local government agency that is 
required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code in accordance with provisions of the Act? 
CONCLUSION 
From the facts provided, it appears that the West County Solid Waste Planning Committee 
is a local government agency that is required to adopt a Conflict oflnterest Code. 
FACTS 
You are an analyst for the County of Fresno responsible for helping to reconvene a number 
of solid waste commissions. Fresno County has three regional planning groups for meeting the 
needs of the County and its solid waste: The Southeast Regional Solid Waste Commission, the 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Infonnal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fonnal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 


File No. 1-16-068 
Page No. 2 
West County Solid Waste Planning Committee and the Fresno-Clovis Metropolitan Solid Waste 
Commission. 
The Southeast Regional Solid Waste Commission was created in 1970 to help administer a 
regional landfill in the southeastern area of the County. That Commission has a Conflict of Interest 
Code and its members file annual SEis. In 1977, following the Solid Waste Resource and Recovery 
Acts, the Fresno County Board of Supervisors directed that three joint powers agencies be created 
(two new agencies to join the already established Southeast Regional Solid Waste Commission). 
Similar to the Southeast Regional Solid Waste Commission, the Fresno-Clovis Metropolitan Solid 
Waste Commission has a Conflict oflnterest Code and its members file annual SEis. 
The West County Solid Waste Planning Committee ("Planning Committee"), was 
established in 1987 under a Joint Powers Agreement3 between the County of Fresno and the Cities 
of Mendota, Coalinga, Firebaugh, Kerman, Huron, and San Joaquin. The Planning Committee has 
not convened for several years and its members never filed annual Statements of Economic 
Interests ("SEI"), based allegedly on the notion that it served only in a purely advisory capacity. 
Article I of the JPA explains that the California Solid Waste Management and Resource 
Recovery Act of 1972 was enacted for local agencies to develop a solid waste management plan for 
each county, including not less than a majority of the cities within the county. It further states the 
public interest requires the parties under the JP A to develop and implement such a plan. 
Article III of the JP A establishes the Planning Committee and lists its duties: 1) Plan for the 
transfer, disposition, and resource recovery of solid waste within the planning area; 2) Advise the 
Fresno County Board of Supervisors on all matters relating to solid waste disposal and processing 
in the planning area; 3) Provide a forum before whom residents in the West Fresno County area 
may express their desires on matters relating to solid waste disposal and recovery; and 4) 
Implement the provisions of the Fresno County Solid Waste Management Plan. 
The Planning Committee consists of eight persons: two supervisors from the Fresno County 
Board of Supervisors and one councilmember from each of the six participating Cities. The JPA 
states that the Planning Committee's secretary is responsible for submitting a budget for planning, 
implementation, and coordination activities to be approved by the Planning Committee. In addition, 
the Planning Committee can determine to undertake capital projects unrelated to the disposal 
facilities or its stated functions. And it has the authority to order Fresno County's Auditor­
Controllerffreasurer to disburse monies to fund the Committee's budget. 
ANALYSIS 
The Commission is not the Code Reviewing Body for the Planning Committee; however, 
pursuant to Regulation 18329.5( c): 
"The Commission may, upon request, provide advice or technical 
assistance to a party for the purpose of determining whether that party is 
an agency as defined in [Sections] 82041 and 82049, and is therefore 
3 The 1987 JPA amended (and superseded) the 1977 JPA. 


File No. 1-16-068 
Page No. 3 
required to adopt and promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to 
Government Code Section 87300. The Commission may also, upon 
request, provide advice or assistance to an agency concerning which 
positions should be designated in the agency's Conflict of Interest Code 
through application of[Regulation] 18701." 
Your question essentially concedes the Planning Committee is a local government agency. 
And generally, as a local government agency, the Planning Committee is required to adopt a 
Conflict of Interest Code that sets forth appropriate financial disclosure categories for its members 
and any employees or consultants who engage in governmental decision-making. (Sections 87300 
and 87302.) 
However, as a local government agency, the Planning Committee is only required to adopt a 
Conflict of Interest Code to the extent that its members, employees, or consultants are actually 
engaged in governmental decisionmaking. Under Section 87100, the Act's conflict of interest 
provisions apply only to "public officials," prohibiting officials from "making," "participating in 
making" or using their official positions to "influence" a governmental decision in which they have 
a financial interest. As defined by Section 82048 and Regulation 18701, "public official" is "every 
member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency." Members of the 
Planning Committee are public officials under the Act. 
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the 
authority of his or her office or position, authorizes or directs any action, votes on a matter, appoints 
a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any 
contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18704(a).) A public official 
"participates in a governmental decision" when he or she provides information, an opinion, or a 
recommendation in order to affect the decision without significant intervening substantive review. 
(Regulation 18704(b).) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence 
a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any member, 
officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. {Regulation 18704( c ). ) 
Additionally, for purposes of members of a committee such as the Planning Committee, 
Regulation 18700 provides the following: 
"(c) For purposes of the Act's conflict of interest prohibitions (Sections 
87100-87105; Regulations 18700-18707), the following definitions 
apply: 
"(2) "Member" does not include an individual who performs duties as 
part of a committee4, board, commission, group, or other body that 
does not have decisionmaking authority. 
4 You had questioned whether a group formed as a "committee" automatically puts it within the category of 
being purely advisory. As Regulation 18700 makes clear, any group or body, including a committee, can possess 
decisionmaking authority. 


File No. 1-16-068 
Page No. 4 
"(A) A committee, board, commission, group, or other body possesses 
decisionmaking authority whenever: 
"(i) It may make a final governmental decision; 
"(ii) It may compel or prevent a governmental decision either 
by reason of an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by 
reason of a veto that may not be overridden; or 
"(iii) It makes substantive recommendations and, over an 
extended period of time, those recommendations have been 
regularly approved without significant amendment 
or 
modification by another public official or governmental agency. 
"(B) A committee, board, commission, group, or other body does not 
possess decisionrnaking authority if it is formed or engaged for the sole 
purpose of researching a topic and preparing a report or 
recommendation for submission to another public official or 
governmental agency that has final decisionmaking authority, and does 
not meet any of the criteria set forth in subsection (2)(A)(i-iii)." 
Under your facts, the JPA that established the Planning Committee provides that it has the 
duty not only to ''plan for the transfer, disposition, and resource recovery of solid waste" within the 
specified jurisdiction, but also to "implement the provisions of the Fresno County Solid Waste 
Management Plan." These duties are in addition to its express responsibility to advise the Fresno 
County Board of Supervisors on all matters relating to solid waste disposal. Moreover, the 
Committee's secretary is charged with submitting a budget for planning, implementation and 
coordination activities that must be approved by the Committee. And the Committee can decide to 
undertake capital projects unrelated to the disposal facilities or its stated functions. Finally, the 
Committee has the authority to order Fresno County's Auditor-Controller/Treasurer to disburse 
monies to fund the Committee's budget. 
The Committee's duties and authority as expressed in the JPA evince an intent that the 
Planning Committee have more than a simple duty to advise. Indeed, it appears the JPA provides 
the Planning Committee with decisionrnaking authority. And this conclusion is only bolstered by 
the fact that the other two regional planning groups, the Fresno-Clovis Metropolitan Solid Waste 
Commission and the Southeast Regional Solid Waste Commission, have Conflict of Interest Codes 
that require members to file SEis and to comply with the Act's conflict of interest provisions. 
However, this is ultimately a determination to be made by the Fresno County Board of Supervisors, 
the appropriate code reviewing body. (See Sections 87301 and 8201 I.) 


JW:jgl 
File No. 1-16-068 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
0 
Co nsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Is the West County Solid Waste Planning Committee a local government agency that is 
required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code in accordance with provisions of the Act?
sections.conclusion:
From the facts provided, it appears that the West County Solid Waste Planning Committee 
is a local government agency that is required to adopt a Conflict oflnterest Code.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is the West County Solid Waste Planning Committee a local government agency that is 
required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code in accordance with provisions of the Act?

CONCLUSION: From the facts provided, it appears that the West County Solid Waste Planning Committee 
is a local government agency that is required to adopt a Conflict oflnterest Code.
